new file mode 100644
index 0000000..1ec5eb7
--- /dev/null
+++ b/CLAUDE.md
@@ -0,0 +1,34 @@
+# Project Guidelines
+
+## Architecture
+- This project follows the repository pattern for data access
+- Services depend on repositories, never on each other directly
+- Events are preferred over direct cross-service calls for decoupling
+- All business logic lives in the service layer
+
+## Coding Conventions
+- Use TypeScript strict mode — no `any` types
+- All public methods must have JSDoc comments
+- Use dependency injection via constructor parameters
+- Error handling: throw typed errors, never return null for errors
+- Prefer async/await over raw Promises
+
+## Testing
+- Tests use vitest
+- Each module has a corresponding test file in tests/
+- Mock external dependencies, test business logic directly
+- Minimum: test the happy path and one error path per public method
+
+## File Organization
+- src/models/ — Data models and interfaces
+- src/repositories/ — Data access layer (implements repository interfaces)
+- src/services/ — Business logic layer
+- src/events/ — Event definitions and event bus
+- src/utils/ — Shared utilities (database, logger, pagination)
+- src/config/ — Configuration files
+- tests/ — Test files mirroring src/ structure
+
+## Git Practices
+- Commit atomically per logical change
+- Write descriptive commit messages explaining the "why"
+- Run tests before committing

diff --git a/DESIGN.md b/DESIGN.md
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..85b0d20
--- /dev/null
+++ b/DESIGN.md
@@ -0,0 +1,113 @@
+# Analytics Dashboard — Design Document
+
+## Overview
+
+The analytics dashboard provides read-only aggregated views over existing Order and User data. It does not introduce new mutable state — all metrics are computed on demand from the existing repositories.
+
+## Route Structure
+
+| Method | Path | Handler | Description |
+|--------|------|---------|-------------|
+| GET | `/api/analytics/dashboard` | `getDashboard` | Full dashboard payload — all metrics, breakdowns, and user analytics in one request |
+| GET | `/api/analytics/metrics/:type` | `getMetric` | Single metric with time series. `:type` is one of `orders`, `revenue`, `users`, `average-order-value` |
+| GET | `/api/analytics/orders/status` | `getOrderStatus` | Order status distribution (pie chart data) |
+| GET | `/api/analytics/revenue` | `getRevenue` | Revenue breakdown by order status |
+| GET | `/api/analytics/users` | `getUserAnalytics` | User growth, activity, and role distribution |
+
+**Why this structure:**
+- The `/dashboard` endpoint bundles everything so the frontend can render the full view in one request — avoids waterfall loading.
+- Individual endpoints (`/metrics/:type`, `/orders/status`, etc.) exist for partial refreshes and drill-down views. Order statuses change frequently, so the frontend can poll `/orders/status` without re-fetching all metrics.
+- All endpoints share the same `AnalyticsQuery` parameters (startDate, endDate, granularity) for consistency.
+
+## Data Model Design
+
+### Query Types
+- **`AnalyticsQuery`** — Base query with date range and optional `TimeGranularity` (`hour | day | week | month`).
+- **`MetricQuery`** — Extends `AnalyticsQuery` with a `MetricType` selector.
+
+### Response Types
+- **`MetricSummary`** — A single metric card: current value, previous-period value, percent change, trend direction, and a time series for charting.
+- **`TimeSeriesPoint`** — `{ timestamp, value, label? }` for each bucket in a time series.
+- **`OrderStatusBreakdown`** — Status, count, and percentage of total.
+- **`RevenueBreakdown`** — Total revenue, average order value, and revenue by order status.
+- **`UserAnalytics`** — Total users, new/active in period, breakdown by role.
+- **`DashboardData`** — Composite response bundling all of the above.
+- **`AnalyticsResponse<T>`** — Standard envelope: `{ success, data, meta: { generatedAt, queryDurationMs } }`.
+
+### Why these models:
+- `MetricSummary` with `previousValue`/`changePercent` supports comparison cards (e.g., "Revenue up 12% vs last period") without a second API call.
+- `TimeSeriesPoint` is intentionally minimal — the frontend chooses how to render (line chart, bar chart, etc.).
+- The `AnalyticsResponse<T>` wrapper keeps all responses uniform and includes timing metadata for observability.
+
+## Aggregation Service Design
+
+### `AnalyticsService`
+
+Located at `src/services/analytics.service.ts`. Follows the existing service pattern:
+- Constructor-injected dependencies (`OrderRepository`, `UserRepository`)
+- Async methods returning typed results
+- Uses `Logger` for observability
+
+**How it should work (for the implementing developer):**
+
+1. **`getDashboard(query)`** — Orchestrator. Calls all other methods in parallel via `Promise.all` and assembles the composite `DashboardData`.
+
+2. **`getMetricSummary(query)`** — Core aggregation method. For each metric type:
+   - `orders`: Count orders where `createdAt` is in the query date range.
+   - `revenue`: Sum `order.total` for orders in the range.
+   - `users`: Count users where `createdAt` is in the range.
+   - `average-order-value`: Revenue divided by order count.
+   - For the `previousValue`, compute the same metric for an equivalent-length period immediately before `startDate`.
+   - Build `timeSeries` by bucketing records into intervals based on `granularity`.
+
+3. **`getOrderStatusBreakdown(query)`** — Filter orders by date range, group by `status`, compute count and percentage.
+
+4. **`getRevenueBreakdown(query)`** — Filter orders by date range, sum totals by status, compute average.
+
+5. **`getUserAnalytics(query)`** — Count total users, filter by `createdAt` for new users, cross-reference with orders for active users, group by role.
+
+### Suggested utility: Time bucketing
+
+A reusable helper in `src/utils/time-series.ts` would be useful:
+
+```typescript
+function bucketByTime<T>(
+  items: T[],
+  getTimestamp: (item: T) => Date,
+  getValue: (item: T) => number,
+  granularity: TimeGranularity,
+  startDate: Date,
+  endDate: Date,
+): TimeSeriesPoint[]
+```
+
+This generates a complete series (with zero-filled gaps) between start and end dates, which is important for charting.
+
+## Architectural Decisions
+
+1. **Pull-based, not event-driven.** Unlike the NotificationService (which subscribes to events), the AnalyticsService queries repositories on demand. Rationale: analytics queries span arbitrary time ranges, so pre-aggregating from events would require maintaining a separate data store. The pull-based approach is simpler and correct for the current in-memory scale. If performance becomes an issue, a caching layer can be added in front of the service without changing the API.
+
+2. **No new repository.** Analytics doesn't have its own persisted data, so it reads from `OrderRepository` and `UserRepository` directly. If caching is added later, it would go in the service layer, not a repository.
+
+3. **Route handlers as typed functions.** Since TaskFlow Pro has no HTTP framework, routes are implemented as a factory function (`createAnalyticsRoutes`) that returns an object of typed async handlers. This makes them easy to wire into any future HTTP layer (Express, Fastify, etc.) or to call directly in tests.
+
+4. **Response envelope (`AnalyticsResponse<T>`).** All routes return a uniform wrapper with `success`, `data`, and `meta` fields. This is forward-compatible with error handling (a failed response could set `success: false` with an `error` field).
+
+5. **Previous-period comparison built into `MetricSummary`.** Rather than requiring two API calls (current period + previous period), each metric summary includes both values. The previous period is always the same duration as the queried period, immediately preceding `startDate`.
+
+## File Inventory
+
+| File | Purpose |
+|------|---------|
+| `src/models/analytics.ts` | All analytics TypeScript interfaces and types |
+| `src/services/analytics.service.ts` | Aggregation logic (stub implementations with TODO markers) |
+| `src/routes/analytics.routes.ts` | Route handler factory with input validation |
+| `src/index.ts` | Updated barrel exports for all analytics types and classes |
+
+## Next Steps
+
+1. **Implement `AnalyticsService` methods** — Replace stubs with real aggregation logic. See the implementation guide in the service file header.
+2. **Add `src/utils/time-series.ts`** — Time bucketing utility for building `TimeSeriesPoint[]` arrays.
+3. **Add date-range filtering to repositories** — `OrderRepository` and `UserRepository` may need `findByDateRange(start, end)` methods (or use the existing `findAll` with a predicate).
+4. **Write tests** — Mirror existing test structure in `tests/services/analytics.service.test.ts` and `tests/routes/analytics.routes.test.ts`.
+5. **Add error handling** — The route handlers currently throw on invalid input; consider returning `{ success: false, error }` responses instead.

diff --git a/src/routes/analytics.routes.ts b/src/routes/analytics.routes.ts
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..a74b563
--- /dev/null
+++ b/src/routes/analytics.routes.ts
@@ -0,0 +1,161 @@
+/**
+ * Analytics route handlers — API layer for analytics dashboard.
+ *
+ * Since TaskFlow Pro does not use an HTTP framework, these route handlers
+ * are defined as typed functions with explicit request/response shapes.
+ * They follow the same pattern as the service layer: receive typed input,
+ * delegate to AnalyticsService, return typed output.
+ *
+ * Route structure:
+ *
+ *   GET /api/analytics/dashboard
+ *     → getDashboardHandler(query) → AnalyticsResponse<DashboardData>
+ *     Full dashboard payload with all metric summaries, breakdowns,
+ *     and user analytics. This is the primary endpoint for the dashboard UI.
+ *
+ *   GET /api/analytics/metrics/:type
+ *     → getMetricHandler(query) → AnalyticsResponse<MetricSummary>
+ *     Individual metric with time series. Used for drill-down views
+ *     or when the frontend needs to refresh a single card.
+ *
+ *   GET /api/analytics/orders/status
+ *     → getOrderStatusHandler(query) → AnalyticsResponse<OrderStatusBreakdown[]>
+ *     Order status distribution. Separated so the frontend can poll
+ *     this independently (order statuses change more frequently).
+ *
+ *   GET /api/analytics/revenue
+ *     → getRevenueHandler(query) → AnalyticsResponse<RevenueBreakdown>
+ *     Revenue breakdown by status. Useful for finance-focused views.
+ *
+ *   GET /api/analytics/users
+ *     → getUserAnalyticsHandler(query) → AnalyticsResponse<UserAnalytics>
+ *     User growth, activity, and role distribution.
+ *
+ * All handlers accept an AnalyticsQuery (or MetricQuery) and return an
+ * AnalyticsResponse<T> wrapper. The wrapper includes generation timestamp
+ * and query duration for observability.
+ */
+
+import type { AnalyticsService } from '../services/analytics.service.js';
+import type {
+  AnalyticsQuery,
+  AnalyticsResponse,
+  DashboardData,
+  MetricQuery,
+  MetricSummary,
+  MetricType,
+  OrderStatusBreakdown,
+  RevenueBreakdown,
+  UserAnalytics,
+} from '../models/analytics.js';
+
+/**
+ * Wrap a service call in the standard AnalyticsResponse envelope,
+ * measuring query duration automatically.
+ */
+async function wrapResponse<T>(fn: () => Promise<T>): Promise<AnalyticsResponse<T>> {
+  const start = Date.now();
+  const data = await fn();
+  return {
+    success: true,
+    data,
+    meta: {
+      generatedAt: new Date(),
+      queryDurationMs: Date.now() - start,
+    },
+  };
+}
+
+/**
+ * Validate that a string is a supported MetricType.
+ */
+function isValidMetricType(value: string): value is MetricType {
+  return ['orders', 'revenue', 'users', 'average-order-value'].includes(value);
+}
+
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// Route handler factory
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+/**
+ * Creates all analytics route handlers, bound to a given AnalyticsService.
+ *
+ * Usage:
+ *   const analyticsService = new AnalyticsService(orderRepo, userRepo);
+ *   const routes = createAnalyticsRoutes(analyticsService);
+ *   const dashboard = await routes.getDashboard({ startDate, endDate });
+ */
+export function createAnalyticsRoutes(service: AnalyticsService) {
+  return {
+    /**
+     * GET /api/analytics/dashboard
+     *
+     * Returns the full dashboard payload.
+     */
+    async getDashboard(
+      query: AnalyticsQuery,
+    ): Promise<AnalyticsResponse<DashboardData>> {
+      return wrapResponse(() => service.getDashboard(query));
+    },
+
+    /**
+     * GET /api/analytics/metrics/:type
+     *
+     * Returns a single metric summary with time series.
+     * @param metricType - one of: orders, revenue, users, average-order-value
+     */
+    async getMetric(
+      metricType: string,
+      query: AnalyticsQuery,
+    ): Promise<AnalyticsResponse<MetricSummary>> {
+      if (!isValidMetricType(metricType)) {
+        throw new Error(
+          `Invalid metric type: "${metricType}". ` +
+          `Valid types: orders, revenue, users, average-order-value`,
+        );
+      }
+
+      const metricQuery: MetricQuery = { ...query, metricType };
+      return wrapResponse(() => service.getMetricSummary(metricQuery));
+    },
+
+    /**
+     * GET /api/analytics/orders/status
+     *
+     * Returns order status distribution.
+     */
+    async getOrderStatus(
+      query: AnalyticsQuery,
+    ): Promise<AnalyticsResponse<OrderStatusBreakdown[]>> {
+      return wrapResponse(() => service.getOrderStatusBreakdown(query));
+    },
+
+    /**
+     * GET /api/analytics/revenue
+     *
+     * Returns revenue breakdown.
+     */
+    async getRevenue(
+      query: AnalyticsQuery,
+    ): Promise<AnalyticsResponse<RevenueBreakdown>> {
+      return wrapResponse(() => service.getRevenueBreakdown(query));
+    },
+
+    /**
+     * GET /api/analytics/users
+     *
+     * Returns user analytics.
+     */
+    async getUserAnalytics(
+      query: AnalyticsQuery,
+    ): Promise<AnalyticsResponse<UserAnalytics>> {
+      return wrapResponse(() => service.getUserAnalytics(query));
+    },
+  };
+}
+
+/**
+ * Type of the route handler object returned by createAnalyticsRoutes.
+ * Useful for consumers that need to reference the handler signatures.
+ */
+export type AnalyticsRoutes = ReturnType<typeof createAnalyticsRoutes>;

diff --git a/src/services/analytics.service.ts b/src/services/analytics.service.ts
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..f2fff7b
--- /dev/null
+++ b/src/services/analytics.service.ts
@@ -0,0 +1,171 @@
+/**
+ * Analytics service — aggregation logic for the analytics dashboard.
+ *
+ * Depends on:
+ * - OrderRepository (data layer) for order data
+ * - UserRepository (data layer) for user data
+ *
+ * Architecture: This is a read-only service. It does not mutate any data.
+ * It queries the existing repositories and computes aggregations over
+ * the results. It does NOT subscribe to the EventBus — it pulls data
+ * on demand rather than maintaining a running aggregate.
+ *
+ * ============================================================
+ * IMPLEMENTATION GUIDE FOR NEXT DEVELOPER
+ * ============================================================
+ *
+ * Each public method currently returns placeholder data. To implement:
+ *
+ * 1. getDashboard():
+ *    - Call getMetricSummary() for each of the four metric types
+ *    - Call getOrderStatusBreakdown() and getRevenueBreakdown()
+ *    - Call getUserAnalytics()
+ *    - Assemble into a DashboardData object
+ *
+ * 2. getMetricSummary(query):
+ *    - Fetch orders/users from repositories filtered by query date range
+ *    - Compute the current-period value (e.g., count of orders, sum of revenue)
+ *    - Compute the previous-period value (same duration, shifted back)
+ *    - Build time series by bucketing records into the requested granularity
+ *    - Calculate changePercent and trend
+ *
+ * 3. getOrderStatusBreakdown(query):
+ *    - Fetch orders in the date range
+ *    - Group by status, count each, compute percentages
+ *
+ * 4. getRevenueBreakdown(query):
+ *    - Fetch orders in the date range
+ *    - Sum totals overall and by status
+ *    - Compute average order value
+ *
+ * 5. getUserAnalytics(query):
+ *    - Fetch users, filter by createdAt for new users
+ *    - Cross-reference with orders to find active users
+ *    - Group by role
+ *
+ * Time bucketing helper: Consider adding a utility in src/utils/
+ * that buckets an array of dated records into TimeSeriesPoint[].
+ * ============================================================
+ */
+
+import type { OrderRepository } from '../repositories/order.repository.js';
+import type { UserRepository } from '../repositories/user.repository.js';
+import type {
+  AnalyticsQuery,
+  MetricQuery,
+  MetricSummary,
+  DashboardData,
+  OrderStatusBreakdown,
+  RevenueBreakdown,
+  UserAnalytics,
+} from '../models/analytics.js';
+import { Logger } from '../utils/logger.js';
+
+export class AnalyticsService {
+  private logger = new Logger('AnalyticsService');
+
+  constructor(
+    private orderRepository: OrderRepository,
+    private userRepository: UserRepository,
+  ) {}
+
+  /**
+   * Get the full dashboard data for a given time range.
+   *
+   * This is the primary entry point for the dashboard route.
+   * It assembles all metrics into a single DashboardData response.
+   */
+  async getDashboard(query: AnalyticsQuery): Promise<DashboardData> {
+    this.logger.info(
+      `Generating dashboard data: ${query.startDate.toISOString()} to ${query.endDate.toISOString()}`,
+    );
+
+    const granularity = query.granularity ?? 'day';
+    const resolvedQuery: AnalyticsQuery = { ...query, granularity };
+
+    // TODO: Replace placeholders with real aggregation (see implementation guide above)
+    const [orders, revenue, users, averageOrderValue] = await Promise.all([
+      this.getMetricSummary({ ...resolvedQuery, metricType: 'orders' }),
+      this.getMetricSummary({ ...resolvedQuery, metricType: 'revenue' }),
+      this.getMetricSummary({ ...resolvedQuery, metricType: 'users' }),
+      this.getMetricSummary({ ...resolvedQuery, metricType: 'average-order-value' }),
+    ]);
+
+    return {
+      query: resolvedQuery,
+      metrics: { orders, revenue, users, averageOrderValue },
+      orderStatusBreakdown: await this.getOrderStatusBreakdown(resolvedQuery),
+      revenueBreakdown: await this.getRevenueBreakdown(resolvedQuery),
+      userAnalytics: await this.getUserAnalytics(resolvedQuery),
+      generatedAt: new Date(),
+    };
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Get a summary for a single metric type over a time range.
+   */
+  async getMetricSummary(query: MetricQuery): Promise<MetricSummary> {
+    this.logger.info(`Computing metric summary: ${query.metricType}`);
+
+    // TODO: Implement real aggregation from repositories
+    return {
+      metricType: query.metricType,
+      currentValue: 0,
+      previousValue: 0,
+      changePercent: 0,
+      trend: 'flat',
+      timeSeries: [],
+    };
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Get order count breakdown by status for a time range.
+   */
+  async getOrderStatusBreakdown(query: AnalyticsQuery): Promise<OrderStatusBreakdown[]> {
+    this.logger.info('Computing order status breakdown');
+
+    // TODO: Implement real aggregation from OrderRepository
+    return [
+      { status: 'pending', count: 0, percentage: 0 },
+      { status: 'confirmed', count: 0, percentage: 0 },
+      { status: 'shipped', count: 0, percentage: 0 },
+      { status: 'delivered', count: 0, percentage: 0 },
+      { status: 'cancelled', count: 0, percentage: 0 },
+    ];
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Get revenue breakdown for a time range.
+   */
+  async getRevenueBreakdown(query: AnalyticsQuery): Promise<RevenueBreakdown> {
+    this.logger.info('Computing revenue breakdown');
+
+    // TODO: Implement real aggregation from OrderRepository
+    return {
+      totalRevenue: 0,
+      averageOrderValue: 0,
+      byStatus: [
+        { status: 'pending', revenue: 0 },
+        { status: 'confirmed', revenue: 0 },
+        { status: 'shipped', revenue: 0 },
+        { status: 'delivered', revenue: 0 },
+        { status: 'cancelled', revenue: 0 },
+      ],
+    };
+  }
+
+  /**
+   * Get user analytics for a time range.
+   */
+  async getUserAnalytics(query: AnalyticsQuery): Promise<UserAnalytics> {
+    this.logger.info('Computing user analytics');
+
+    // TODO: Implement real aggregation from UserRepository and OrderRepository
+    return {
+      totalUsers: 0,
+      newUsersInPeriod: 0,
+      activeUsersInPeriod: 0,
+      byRole: [],
+    };
+  }
+}

You are Agent 1 of 5 working on a codebase at /var/folders/45/myq5db_n6q5cyhtb39vkbmp80000gn/T/twining-bench-jbDERT.

## Codebase Orientation
This is a TypeScript service (~1,140 lines, ~24 files). You'll be adding a new analytics feature. Follow existing patterns:
- `src/services/` — existing service classes (follow this pattern for your analytics service)
- `src/models/` — existing model/type definitions (put analytics types here)
- `src/routes/` or `src/controllers/` — existing route handlers (if present, follow their pattern)
- `tests/` — existing test files

Directory structure:
```
src/
  models/        # Data models
  services/      # Business logic
  repositories/  # Data access layer
  utils/         # Shared utilities
tests/           # Test files
```

Your task: Design and scaffold the API for a new analytics dashboard. Create the route structure, data models, and write a brief design doc.

Specifically:
1. Design the analytics dashboard API: decide on routes (e.g., GET /api/analytics/dashboard, GET /api/analytics/metrics/:type), request/response shapes, and data flow.
2. Create TypeScript interfaces/types for analytics data models (e.g., DashboardData, MetricSummary, TimeSeriesPoint) in `src/models/` or `src/types/`.
3. Scaffold the route files with stub handlers that return placeholder data.
4. Write a brief design document (DESIGN.md or inline comments) explaining:
   - The route structure and why
   - The data model design
   - How the aggregation service should work (for the next developer)
   - Any architectural decisions you made
5. Make sure the scaffolded code compiles.

Important:
- Focus on DESIGN over implementation — the next sessions will implement the actual logic.
- Create clear interfaces that downstream sessions can implement against.
- Document your decisions thoroughly — 4 more developers will build on your foundation.

parameters.content:
# Analytics Dashboard — Design Document

## Overview

The analytics dashboard provides read-only aggregated views over existing Order and User data. It does not introduce new mutable state — all metrics are computed on demand from the existing repositories.

## Route Structure

| Method | Path | Handler | Description |
|--------|------|---------|-------------|
| GET | `/api/analytics/dashboard` | `getDashboard` | Full dashboard payload — all metrics, breakdowns, and user analytics in one request |
| GET | `/api/analytics/metrics/:type` | `getMetric` | Single metric with time series. `:type` is one of `orders`, `revenue`, `users`, `average-order-value` |
| GET | `/api/analytics/orders/status` | `getOrderStatus` | Order status distribution (pie chart data) |
| GET | `/api/analytics/revenue` | `getRevenue` | Revenue breakdown by order status |
| GET | `/api/analytics/users` | `getUserAnalytics` | User growth, activity, and role distribution |

**Why this structure:**
- The `/dashboard` endpoint bundles everything so the frontend can render the full view in one request — avoids waterfall loading.
- Individual endpoints (`/metrics/:type`, `/orders/status`, etc.) exist for partial refreshes and drill-down views. Order statuses change frequently, so the frontend can poll `/orders/status` without re-fetching all metrics.
- All endpoints share the same `AnalyticsQuery` parameters (startDate, endDate, granularity) for consistency.

## Data Model Design

### Query Types
- **`AnalyticsQuery`** — Base query with date range and optional `TimeGranularity` (`hour | day | week | month`).
- **`MetricQuery`** — Extends `AnalyticsQuery` with a `MetricType` selector.

### Response Types
- **`MetricSummary`** — A single metric card: current value, previous-period value, percent change, trend direction, and a time series for charting.
- **`TimeSeriesPoint`** — `{ timestamp, value, label? }` for each bucket in a time series.
- **`OrderStatusBreakdown`** — Status, count, and percentage of total.
- **`RevenueBreakdown`** — Total revenue, average order value, and revenue by order status.
- **`UserAnalytics`** — Total users, new/active in period, breakdown by role.
- **`DashboardData`** — Composite response bundling all of the above.
- **`AnalyticsResponse<T>`** — Standard envelope: `{ success, data, meta: { generatedAt, queryDurationMs } }`.

### Why these models:
- `MetricSummary` with `previousValue`/`changePercent` supports comparison cards (e.g., "Revenue up 12% vs last period") without a second API call.
- `TimeSeriesPoint` is intentionally minimal — the frontend chooses how to render (line chart, bar chart, etc.).
- The `AnalyticsResponse<T>` wrapper keeps all responses uniform and includes timing metadata for observability.

## Aggregation Service Design

### `AnalyticsService`

Located at `src/services/analytics.service.ts`. Follows the existing service pattern:
- Constructor-injected dependencies (`OrderRepository`, `UserRepository`)
- Async methods returning typed results
- Uses `Logger` for observability

**How it should work (for the implementing developer):**

1. **`getDashboard(query)`** — Orchestrator. Calls all other methods in parallel via `Promise.all` and assembles the composite `DashboardData`.

2. **`getMetricSummary(query)`** — Core aggregation method. For each metric type:
   - `orders`: Count orders where `createdAt` is in the query date range.
   - `revenue`: Sum `order.total` for orders in the range.
   - `users`: Count users where `createdAt` is in the range.
   - `average-order-value`: Revenue divided by order count.
   - For the `previousValue`, compute the same metric for an equivalent-length period immediately before `startDate`.
   - Build `timeSeries` by bucketing records into intervals based on `granularity`.

3. **`getOrderStatusBreakdown(query)`** — Filter orders by date range, group by `status`, compute count and percentage.

4. **`getRevenueBreakdown(query)`** — Filter orders by date range, sum totals by status, compute average.

5. **`getUserAnalytics(query)`** — Count total users, filter by `createdAt` for new users, cross-reference with orders for active users, group by role.

### Suggested utility: Time bucketing

A reusable helper in `src/utils/time-series.ts` would be useful:

```typescript
function bucketByTime<T>(
  items: T[],
  getTimestamp: (item: T) => Date,
  getValue: (item: T) => number,
  granularity: TimeGranularity,
  startDate: Date,
  endDate: Date,
): TimeSeriesPoint[]
```

This generates a complete series (with zero-filled gaps) between start and end dates, which is important for charting.

## Architectural Decisions

1. **Pull-based, not event-driven.** Unlike the NotificationService (which subscribes to events), the AnalyticsService queries repositories on demand. Rationale: analytics queries span arbitrary time ranges, so pre-aggregating from events would require maintaining a separate data store. The pull-based approach is simpler and correct for the current in-memory scale. If performance becomes an issue, a caching layer can be added in front of the service without changing the API.

2. **No new repository.** Analytics doesn't have its own persisted data, so it reads from `OrderRepository` and `UserRepository` directly. If caching is added later, it would go in the service layer, not a repository.

3. **Route handlers as typed functions.** Since TaskFlow Pro has no HTTP framework, routes are implemented as a factory function (`createAnalyticsRoutes`) that returns an object of typed async handlers. This makes them easy to wire into any future HTTP layer (Express, Fastify, etc.) or to call directly in tests.

4. **Response envelope (`AnalyticsResponse<T>`).** All routes return a uniform wrapper with `success`, `data`, and `meta` fields. This is forward-compatible with error handling (a failed response could set `success: false` with an `error` field).

5. **Previous-period comparison built into `MetricSummary`.** Rather than requiring two API calls (current period + previous period), each metric summary includes both values. The previous period is always the same duration as the queried period, immediately preceding `startDate`.

## File Inventory

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `src/models/analytics.ts` | All analytics TypeScript interfaces and types |
| `src/services/analytics.service.ts` | Aggregation logic (stub implementations with TODO markers) |
| `src/routes/analytics.routes.ts` | Route handler factory with input validation |
| `src/index.ts` | Updated barrel exports for all analytics types and classes |

## Next Steps

1. **Implement `AnalyticsService` methods** — Replace stubs with real aggregation logic. See the implementation guide in the service file header.
2. **Add `src/utils/time-series.ts`** — Time bucketing utility for building `TimeSeriesPoint[]` arrays.
3. **Add date-range filtering to repositories** — `OrderRepository` and `UserRepository` may need `findByDateRange(start, end)` methods (or use the existing `findAll` with a predicate).
4. **Write tests** — Mirror existing test structure in `tests/services/analytics.service.test.ts` and `tests/routes/analytics.routes.test.ts`.
5. **Add error handling** — The route handlers currently throw on invalid input; consider returning `{ success: false, error }` responses instead.
